coredata.dc:title: Preparation of pH-resistant multicolor thiol fluorescent carbon dot using cysteine and neutral red as precursors and its application in detection and adsorption of apigenin
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Qingyan
Ma, Shujuan
Yan, Mingjia
Wang, Jinshu
Wang, Xiaoqiong
Ou, Junjie
coredata.dc:description: Conventional carbon dots (CDs) typically exhibit substantial variations in fluorescence intensity across different pH. This limitation underscores the pressing need for advancements in their stability and performance under diverse environmental conditions. Herein, l-cysteine and neutral red are selected as precursors, and three kinds of CDs, which can emit red, orange, and green fluorescent light (assigned as r-CD, o-CD, g-CD, respectively), are synthesized by simply changing the reaction conditions. The pH resistance of r-CD and o-CD in acidic and alkaline environments was demonstrated. A fluorescence detection system for natural flavonoid apigenin was established with r-CD, as r-CD exhibited obviously selective quenching of apigenin, without obvious response to other analogs. The linear range of apigenin detection was divided into low concentration at 2.85–30 μmol L−1 and high concentration at 90–225 μmol L−1, demonstrating excellent anti-interference ability in complex environments. In addition, the molecularly imprinted polymer containing r-CD (r-CD@MIP) was also prepared by surface molecular imprinting technique. The maximum adsorption capacity for apigenin was 28.39 mg g−1, and the adsorption equilibrium reached in 90 min. A complete adsorption-fluorescence detection method of apigenin in actual samples was successfully established by using r-CD and r-CD@MIP. The results demonstrated that the developed CDs and detection methods have promising applications in the field of fluorescence sensing and adsorption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
pH resistance
Fluorescence tunability
Apigenin
Molecularly imprinted polymers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Properties pH resistance of multi-color thiol CDs
Reflection of UV absorption on the fluorescence color of CDs
Stability of r-CD and anti-interference ability in detecting apigenin
Possible response mechanism of r-CD to apigenin

Ma, the Key Research and Development Program of Ningxia (No. 2022BFE02002), the CAS-Weigao Research & Development Program ([2017]-009) to J. Ou, and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U2341209) to J. Wang.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic preparation of CDs and r-CD@MIP.
TEM images of (a, b) r-CD and SEM images of (c, d) MAR and (e, f) r-CD@MIP.
(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) XPS full spectra of r-CD, MAR-Br, r-CD@MIP, and (c–f) XPS high-resolution spectra of r-CD.

Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) r-CD, (b) o-CD and (c) g-CD at different excitation wavelength, fluorescence emission spectra of (d) r-CD, (e) o-CD and (f) g-CD at different pH at the maximum excitation wavelength.
(a) UV absorption spectra of r-CD, o-CD, (b) and UV absorption spectra of g-CD at different pH.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra and (b) fluorescence intensity at 630 nm of r-CD in different solutions (250 μmol L−1), (c) fluorescence emission spectra and (c) fluorescence intensity at 630 nm of r-CD in apigenin solutions with different concentrations (0–225 μmol L−1).
Fluorescence intensity of r-CD at the emission wavelength of 630 nm (a) before and after UV irradiation or oxidation and (b) in multicomponent mixtures.
(a) UV absorption spectra of apigenin and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of r-CD, (b) UV absorption spectra of r-CD and r-CD/apigenin.
(a) Adsorption isothermal curves and (b) adsorption kinetic curves of r-CD@MIP and NIP.

head.ce:title: Preparation of pH-resistant multicolor thiol fluorescent carbon dot using cysteine and neutral red as precursors and its application in detection and adsorption of apigenin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory for Chemical Engineering and Technology, State Ethnic Affairs Commission, North Minzu University, Yinchuan 750021, China

Key Laboratory for Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi'an 710127, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi’an 710127, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conventional carbon dots (CDs) typically exhibit substantial variations in fluorescence intensity across different pH. This limitation underscores the pressing need for advancements in their stability and performance under diverse environmental conditions. Herein, l-cysteine and neutral red are selected as precursors, and three kinds of CDs, which can emit red, orange, and green fluorescent light (assigned as r-CD, o-CD, g-CD, respectively), are synthesized by simply changing the reaction conditions. The pH resistance of r-CD and o-CD in acidic and alkaline environments was demonstrated. A fluorescence detection system for natural flavonoid apigenin was established with r-CD, as r-CD exhibited obviously selective quenching of apigenin, without obvious response to other analogs. The linear range of apigenin detection was divided into low concentration at 2.85–30 μmol L−1 and high concentration at 90–225 μmol L−1, demonstrating excellent anti-interference ability in complex environments. In addition, the molecularly imprinted polymer containing r-CD (r-CD@MIP) was also prepared by surface molecular imprinting technique. The maximum adsorption capacity for apigenin was 28.39 mg g−1, and the adsorption equilibrium reached in 90 min. A complete adsorption-fluorescence detection method of apigenin in actual samples was successfully established by using r-CD and r-CD@MIP. The results demonstrated that the developed CDs and detection methods have promising applications in the field of fluorescence sensing and adsorption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are regarded as promising candidate materials based on their excellent optical properties, biocompatibility, and ease of functionalization [1–4]. CDs have attracted great attention in chemical sensors, analysis, medicine, bioimaging, drug delivery, photocatalysis, mechanical sensors, logic gates, and other applications related to safety and countermeasures [5–7]. In order to meet multifaceted needs, the preparation of CDs with multicolor fluorescent emission is a widespread demand. For example, Wei's group prepared CDs using o-phenylenediamine as precursors by a hydrothermal method, exhibiting multicolor fluorescence by dispersing in different solvents [8]. Yu et al. prepared a color-tunable CD with an emission wavelength ranging from 450 to 640 nm CD by using critic acid and thiourea as precursors and adjusting the volume ratio of solvent water and N, N-dimethylformamide [9]. Jia's team prepared color-tunable CDs by hydrothermally treating p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid and o-, m-, or p-phenylenediamines, respectively, and the as-synthesized CDs could emit blue, green, and orange fluorescence [10]. Ding and colleagues employed urea and p-phenylenediamine as the precursors to prepare CDs, which was then separated via silica-based column chromatography. These separated CDs emitted gradient of light from blue to red under UV light [11]. Although these multicolor CDs emerged, there are still few reports on simple fabrication method using the same precursors to exhibit multicolor fluorescence in pure aqueous solution.

As a kind of fluorescent nanomaterial, CDs often demonstrate significant changes in fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength under different pH environments [12,13]. This pH-sensitive property can be attributed to the protonation or deprotonation of the surface functional groups of CDs, the dissociation of either carboxyl group or α-H in β-dicarbonyl group, along with the impact of variations in hydrogen ion concentration on the electron transfer process of CDs, etc [14,15]. Zhang et al. observed that residual –NH2 groups in raw materials or intermediate products (such as o-phenylenediamine and 2,3-diaminophe-nazine) may determine the red fluorescence emission of CDs [16]. In the acidic conditions, the –NH2 groups undergo protonation, resulting in a pronounced red shift of CDs fluorescence. Conversely, under alkaline environment, a blue shift is observed, which corresponds to the deprotonation of the –NH2 functionality. Madonia and collaborators effectively improved the photobleaching resistance of CDs by incorporating dyes into the carbonaceous core, preventing aggregation-induced quenching [17]. This sensitivity to pH value is highly valuable in a few applications, however, it may also lead to serious interference when using CDs for fluorescence detection in complex environments. Therefore, the development of CDs with different functional groups, anti-photobleaching and easy-to-adjust fluorescence colors is more suitable for various applications in complex environments.

Apigenin is a naturally occurring flavonoid compound with multiple pharmacological activities such as anti-inflammatory, anti-toxic and anti-cancer [18–21]. There are several reports on the detection of flavonoid natural products by CDs [22–25]. The detection of apigenin by pH-resistant CDs has attracted our attention. Herein, thiol-containing CDs of various colors (r-CD, o-CD and g-CD) were prepared by using l-cysteine and the neutral red dye as precursors through a one-step hydrothermal method with simply adjusting the reaction conditions. The sensitivity of 3 kinds of CDs to pH changes was measured. A simple method of fluorescence detection of apigenin was established with r-CD, and the fluorescence response of r-CD to various flavonoids and its anti-interference ability in complex environments was developed. Then, combined with molecular imprinting technique, a kind of molecular imprinting material (r-CD@MIP) was prepared by introducing CD into poly(GMA-co-EDMA) resin, and its adsorption performance on apigenin was evaluated by using lake water as an actual sample through isotherm and kinetic adsorption experiments.

The red fluorescent CDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method. Typically, 605 mg of l-cysteine and 578 mg of neutral red were dissolved in 30 mL of 50 % ethanol, and then 0.5 mL of H2SO4 (18 mol L−1) was added dropwise, and ultrasonic mixing was performed. The mixed solution was transferred to an autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 7 h. After the product was cooled to room temperature, it was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and then dialyzed through a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 72 h to obtain red fluorescent CDs (r-CD).

Two other typical fluorescent CDs, namely orange fluorescent CD (o-CD) and green fluorescent CD (g-CD) were obtained by properly adjusting the reaction conditions. The experimental process is shown in the Supporting Information.

According to our previous report [26], monodisperse poly(GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres (noted MAR) were prepared by one-step seeded swelling polymerization. The detailed process is shown in the Supporting Information. Then, MAR was modified in sequence to obtain MAR-Br containing Br group at the end. Typically, 108 mg of apigenin and 161 μL of 4-VP were placed in 15 mL of chloroform/methanol and self-assembled for 30 min. Then 0.75 g MAR-Br, 0.058 g of Bpy, 0.9 mL of EDMA, and 30 mg of concentrated r-CD were placed in a two-necked flask. Under vacuum deoxygenation and nitrogen flow, 0.027 g of CuBr was quickly added. After completion, the reaction was kept at 60 °C for 8 h. After the reaction was completed, the product was filtered and washed with EDTA-2Na, ethanol, and water in turn. The obtained product was extracted with Soxhlet extraction to elute the template, and the mixture of ethanol and glacial acetic acid (8/2, v/v) was used as the eluent for 48 h. After drying, the product CD@MIP was obtained.
The experimental steps of preparing non-molecularly imprinted polymer (NIP) was unchanged except that apigenin was not added.

The fluorescence response of r-CD to apigenin, luteolin, quercetin, and gallic acid solutions was investigated. Among them, apigenin, luteolin and quercetin are all natural flavonoid compounds, and gallic acid is a fragment analogue of these substances. The concentrations of all solutions were 250 μmol L−1. The fluorescence intensity of the mixture was recorded at 630 nm using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with single-wavelength excitation at the maximum excitation wavelength of 270 nm, and the measurements were performed 3 times under the same conditions, and the error bars were plotted.

The relationship between apigenin concentration and r-CD fluorescence intensity was quantitatively analyzed by plotting the r-CD fluorescence intensity versus apigenin concentration relationship. The apigenin concentration was determined to be in the range of 0–25 μmol L−1. A series of mixed solutions containing r-CD and different concentrations of apigenin were prepared. The r-CD concentration was kept constant to maintain the appropriate fluorescence intensity for detection. The fluorescence intensity was measured 3 times and the error bars were plotted.

Quantum yield (Ф F) was measured using quinine sulfate as reference [27]. The quantum yield of r-CD was calculated using the following:
(1)
Φ F
=
Φ R
∫ I
A R
n 2
/ ∫
I R
A
n R 2
where the subscripts F and R refer to the fluorophore and reference compound, respectively, Ф is the fluorescence quantum yield, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength (less than 0.1), ∫I is the integrated fluorescence intensity, and n is the refractive index of the solvents.

The adsorption properties of r-CD@MIP to apigenin were studied by isothermal adsorption experiment and kinetic adsorption experiment, and the adsorption isotherm and kinetic parameters were determined. The adsorption experiment was carried out by UV–visible spectrophotometer.

In the isothermal adsorption experiment, 8 mg of r-CD@MIP/NIP were dispersed in 100 mL of apigenin solution with different concentrations (0–80 mg L−1) and shaken at a constant temperature of 170 rpm for 5 h. After the experiment, the supernatant was centrifuged and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain the adsorbed apigenin solution. The adsorption amount was calculated as follows:
(2)
Q e
=
(
C 0
−
C e
)
V / m
where Q e (mg g−1) is equilibrium adsorption capacity, C 0 and C e (mg L−1) are the initial concentration and equilibrium concentration, respectively, V (mL) is the volume of aqueous solution, and m (mg) is the mass of materials. The isothermal adsorption data were further analyzed by Langmuir and Freundlich isothermal models, whose formulas are as follows:
(3)
C e
/
Q e
=
C e
/
Q max
+ 1 /
K L
Q max
(4)
lg
Q e
= lg
C e
/ n + lg
K F

where Q max (mg g−1) is the maximum adsorption capacity, C e (mg mL−1) is the concentration in equilibrium state, 1/n is the empirical coefficient of Freundlich, K L (mL mg−1) and K F (mg g−1) are Langmuir and Freundlich constant, respectively.

In the kinetic adsorption experiment, 15 mg of r-CD@MIP were dispersed in 100 mL of a 30 mg L−1 apigenin solution and stirred at a constant temperature of 170 rpm for different times (0–240 min). After the experiment, the supernatant was centrifuged and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain the adsorbed apigenin solution. The dynamic adsorption data were further analyzed using pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models, and the formulas are as follows:
(5)
ln
(
Q e
−
Q t
)
= ln
Q e
−
K 1
t
(6)
t /
Q t
= t /
Q e
+ 1 /
K 2
Q e 2
where K 1 and K 2 are the reaction rate constants of pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic model, respectively, and Q t (mg g−1) is the adsorption capacity.

The reliability of adsorption and detection methods was verified through actual sample experiments. The adsorption capability of r-CD@MIP for apigenin in actual samples was measured. Lake water (Ming Lake, Yinchuan, China) was chosen as the sample. After centrifugation, filtration and addition of apigenin, a sample containing 15 mg L−1 of apigenin was obtained. The 15 mg of r-CD@MIP were mixed with 100 mL of the water sample and shaken at room temperature for 3 h. The supernatant was collected, and the apigenin content was determined by fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The synthetic route is shown in Fig. 1 . In the process of synthesizing CDs, l-cysteine was responsible for constructing the carbon core during the reaction and providing active thiol groups to promote the formation and growth of nanostructures, while neutral red was incorporated into the carbon core during the reaction, retaining specific properties. By regulating the reaction, CDs of various colors (r-CD, o-CD and g-CD) can be synthesized by simply changing the reaction conditions under the premise that the ratio of the precursors (l-cysteine, neutral red) remains unchanged. The precipitated impurities are removed by centrifugation and filtration, and the unreacted raw materials and small molecule fluorescent substances are removed by dialysis. Molecular imprinting technology is adopted to prepare polymeric materials with specific recognition capabilities (MIP). This technology forms a cavity or imprint complementary to the target molecule (template molecule) in the polymer network, so that the polymer exhibits a high affinity and selectivity for the target molecule [28–30]. In the process of synthesizing MIP, monodisperse MAR was selected as the matrix, 4-vinylpyridine was utilized as the monomer and EDMA as the cross-linking agent. After adding apigenin, surface atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP) is adopted, which can overcome the problems of embedded and/or heterogeneous distribution of recognition sites [31,32]. The addition of CDs would facilitate to improve the performance of the MIP, and the rich functional groups on the surface of CDs could accelerate the adsorption process, especially when the concentration of the target substance is low [33]. Finally, the r-CD@MIP was fabricated and its performance was measured as following.

The morphology and particle size of r-CD were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2 a–b, r-CD was spherical with a particle size of about 2–3 nm, and clear lattice fringes were visible with a spacing of about 0.21 nm. The surface morphology of MAR-Br and r-CD@MIP was evaluated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), as shown in Fig. 2c–f. MAR-Br was spherical with a particle size of about 4–5 μm and exhibited excellent dispersibility (Fig. 2c and d). The r-CD@MIP prepared by SI-ATRP maintained a similar morphology and size to MAR-Br (Fig. 2e and f), without significantly changing the morphology of MAR-Br.

The type and composition of functional groups on the surface of materials were determined with Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The spectra of CD, MAR-Br and r-CD@MIP are shown in Fig. 3 a, where the vibration peak at 3345 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH in CD, and the broad peak at 3200 cm−1 corresponded to –NH. The characteristic peak at 2640 cm−1 belonged to –SH stretching vibration. The peak at 1610 cm−1 was related to –OH/-NH bending vibration. In summary, these observations demonstrated that CD contains thiol groups and a plethora of amino groups. In the spectra of MAR-Br and CD@MIP, the broad peak at 3500 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of –NH2 and –OH groups. The absorption peak of the –CO group was located at 1730 cm−1. The infrared spectra of them were almost the same, because both were based on MAR as the main structure, and the CD in the imprinting layer and on the surface accounts for a low proportion.

The functional groups and elemental compositions of r-CD, MAR-Br and r-CD@MIP were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and the full XPS spectrum is shown in Fig. 3b. The main elements in r-CD were carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and sulfur, of which carbon content was 73.5 %, oxygen content was 17.9 %, nitrogen content was 6.2 %, and sulfur content was only 2.4 % (Table S1). The carbon element mainly comes from the carbon core of CDs, and nitrogen and sulfur mainly come from functional groups on the surface. The main elements of MAR-Br are carbon (69.5 %), oxygen (28.7 %) and bromine (1.8 %). After the surface of MAR-Br was covered by MIP, the carbon element of r-CD@MIP accounted for 66.0 %, the oxygen element accounted for 32.0 %, the bromine element decreased to 1.4 %, and about 0.7 % of sulfur element was observed. These changes are caused by the imprinting layer and CDs on the surface of MAR-Br via SI-ATRP process. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of r-CD (Fig. 3c) shows three characteristic peaks, corresponding to CO/CN (288.9 eV), C–N (285.2 eV) and C–C/CC (284.2 eV). The N1s spectrum shown in Fig. 3d can be divided into two peaks, locating at 399.2 eV and 398.4 eV. Two peaks at CO (533.6 eV) and C–O (531.9 eV) were observed in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 3e). Four characteristic peaks (168.7, 167.6, 164.8, 163.6 eV) can be observed from the S2p spectrum (Fig. 3f). The XPS results further confirmed that there are a large number of amino, carboxyl and thiol functional groups on the surface of r-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties pH resistance of multi-color thiol CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence properties of three CDs (r-CD, o-CD and g-CD) were tested, as shown in Fig. 4 . The maximum excitation wavelengths of r-CD and o-CD were 270 nm, and the fluorescence emission wavelengths were 630 and 600 nm, respectively (Fig. 4a and b). The maximum excitation wavelength of g-CD was 260 nm, and the fluorescence emission wavelength was 490 nm (Fig. 4c). These results indicated that the fluorescence property of g-CD was significantly different from those of r-CD and o-CD, especially when the environmental pH changes. The properties of three CDs were tested in acidic and alkaline conditions (pH = 3 and pH = 11). The colors of the three CDs solutions shifted slightly toward yellow under acidic conditions, while the colors of them shifted significantly toward yellow under alkaline conditions. This is because some groups on the CD retained the property of neutral red dye [17]. In terms of fluorescence properties, there was no significant change in the fluorescence spectra of r-CD and o-CD (Fig. 4d and e), and their fluorescence properties were stable, demonstrating obvious anti-pH properties. The fluorescence of g-CD showed a certain red shift under acidic conditions (pH = 3), while under alkaline conditions (pH = 11), the fluorescence underwent an obvious red shift (Fig. 4f), and its maximum excitation wavelength changed from 260 to 270 nm. Reports in recent years have pointed out that orange or red fluorescent CDs prepared with ortho-diamino groups or structural analogs as raw materials will have a significant red shift under acidic conditions and a significant blue shift under alkaline conditions. This phenomenon is related to the protonation of the amino groups on the surface of CDs. That is, when the amino groups are protonated, the fluorescence of CDs will red-shift, while the fluorescence will blue-shift when the amino groups are deprotonated [34,35]. Herein, three CDs in this study contain amino groups, but they did not conform to this rule. Among them, r-CD and o-CD exhibted obvious resistance to pH changes, while g-CD possessed red shift in both acidic and alkaline conditions, which was more obvious in alkaline conditions. This indicates that the molecular orbital of g-CD changed, resulting in a decrease in the energy required for electronic transition, thereby shifting the absorption or emission spectrum toward longer wavelengths (red shift). Under alkaline conditions, this change may be more significant, resulting in a more obvious red shift, while r-CD and o-CD did not exhibit this change. The thiol groups on CDs and the neutral red active groups embedded in them might affect the surface electron distribution and energy level structure of CDs. The presence of these groups changed the chemical environment of the CD surface, causing changes in the distribution and transition behavior of electrons on the surface, thereby leading to changes in fluorescence properties. Compared with various amino CDs reported previously, the three CDs exhibit unique optical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reflection of UV absorption on the fluorescence color of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Using the same precursors, the fluorescent color of CDs can be synthesized in a targeted manner by simply adjusting the reaction conditions. The UV absorption spectra of the three CDs were plotted, as shown in Fig. 5 . These CDs all exhibited strong UV absorption peaks at 260–270 nm, which was consistent with the selection of the maximum excitation wavelength mentioned above. g-CD exhibited an obvious UV absorption peak at 400 nm, while o-CD possessed obvious UV absorption peaks at 400 and 500 nm. r-CD similarly exhibited obvious UV absorption peaks at 400 and 530 nm (Fig. 5a and b). It is obvious that the position of UV absorption peak corresponded to the fluorescence color of CDs. By changing the reaction temperature, acidity and doping atoms (P, S), the reaction degree and the structure of CDs can be controlled to synthesize the corresponding thiol-containing CDs with the target fluorescence color. The fluorescence changes of g-CD in acidic and alkaline conditions (pH = 3 and pH = 11) were analyzed, as shown in Fig. 5b. Under acidic conditions, the ultraviolet absorption peak of g-CD at 400 nm showed enhancement and obvious red shift, and the fluorescence changing from green (490 nm) to yellow (530 nm) (Fig. 4f). Under alkaline conditions, strong absorption at 210 nm appeared, indicating that g-CD showed obvious n→π∗ and π→π∗ valence electron transitions under alkaline conditions. Additionly, it offered a more obvious ultraviolet absorption peak at 460 nm, and its fluorescence changed from green (490 nm) to orange-red (610 nm).

The red fluorescence exhibits a strong penetrating property, and there are few reports on red fluorescent CDs. The measured quantum yield of r-CD was 14.0 %. Apigenin, two natural flavonoids (luteolin, quercetin) and fragment analog (gallic acid) were selected to evaluate the fluorescence response of r-CD in solutions with the same concentration (250 μmol L−1). The molecular formulas and UV absorption of 4 compounds are shown in the Supporting Information (Fig. S1). Among them, the molecular formulas and UV absorption spectra of three natural flavonoids were similar, and they all exhibited strong absorption in 250–600 nm. Gallic acid is a fragment analogue, and its UV absorption peak was significantly different. It exhibits strong UV absorption near 265 nm and no UV absorption between 350 and 600 nm. As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, the fluorescence intensity of r-CD in quercetin and gallic acid solutions was 5378 and 5502, respectively, without obvious fluorescence change compared to the blank solution (5968). The fluorescence intensity of r-CD in luteolin solution was 4800, and a certain degree of fluorescence quenching occurred (about 19.6 %). The fluorescence intensity of r-CD in apigenin solution was 2470, and a dramatic fluorescence quenching (about 58.7 %) occurred. This indicates that r-CD exhibited fluorescence selectivity for apigenin and could be utilized to quickly detect apigenin among flavonoid structural analogs.

The fluorescence quenching of r-CD in apigenin solution was quantitatively analyzed, as shown in Fig. 6c and d. As the concentration of apigenin increased, the fluorescence intensity of r-CD gradually decreased. By linear fitting the fluorescence intensity and apigenin concentration (Fig. 6d), it can be observed that the change of the fluorescence intensity of r-CD roughly includes two processes. (i) At low concentrations of apigenin (≤30 μmol L−1), the fluorescence intensity of CD rapidly increased with increasing concentration because there was enough space on the CD surface to interact with apigenin molecules. (ii) As the concentration of apigenin further increased (>30 μmol L−1), most of the fluorescent active groups on the CD surface fully interacted with the apigenin molecules, so the rate at which the fluorescence intensity increased with concentration was low. Using the 3σ rule (σ = S0/S, where S0 is the standard deviation after 11 blank measurements and S is the slope of the fitted curve), the lowest limit of detection (LOD) of apigenin by r-CD was 2.85 μmol L−1. There are two intervals with good linear coefficients, namely the low concentration interval 2.85–30 μmol L−1 (R = 0.998) and the high concentration interval 90–225 μmol L−1 (R = 0.985). Therefore, r-CD can detect apigenin in both low and high concentration ranges, and the detection ability was satisfied at low concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of r-CD and anti-interference ability in detecting apigenin
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence stability of r-CD in complex environments was further studied. The anti-photobleaching performance of r-CD was measured (Fig. 7 a). After r-CDs were continuously irradiated with UV light (365 nm) for 60 min, the fluorescence intensity decreased by about 24 %. When r-CD was placed in H2O2 solution (200 mmol L−1) for 30 min, its fluorescence intensity decreased by about 29 % under strong oxidizing conditions, indicating that r-CD exhibited certain resistance to photobleaching and strong oxidation.

The anti-interference ability and fluorescence selectivity of r-CD for detecting apigenin in complex environments was investigated. The 10 metal ions present in the environment (Na+, K+, Li+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Pd2+, Cd2+, Cu2+ and Fe3+) were selected as interferences, because they likely appear in the actual environment and interfere the detection of apigenin by r-CD. A binary system of apigenin and metal ions was established, in which the apigenin concentration and metal ion concentration were both 25 μmol L−1. As shown in Fig. 7b, when apigenin existed in the environment, r-CD exhibited obvious fluorescence quenching. Compared with apigenin, the effects of metal ions other than Fe3+ on r-CD were relatively small (less than 8 %). Fe3+ caused additional fluorescence quenching of r-CD (17 %). In summary, r-CD can flexibly detect apigenin in a large linear range, and had fluorescence selectivity for apigenin, excellent stability, and can meet the detection of apigenin in most complex environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible response mechanism of r-CD to apigenin
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, possible photoluminescence quenching modes of CDs include photoinduced electron transfer (PET), inner filter effect (IFE), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and dynamic quenching [36–38]. A large degree of spectral overlap was observed between the excitation/emission of r-CD and the absorption of apigenin (Fig. 8 a), suggesting the possible existence of IFE or FRET processes. The fluorescence lifetime of r-CD was analyzed before and after the addition of apigenin. As shown in Fig. S2, in the presence of a lower concentration of apigenin, the fluorescence lifetime of r-CD decreased from 1.43 ns to 1.28 ns. In the FRET process, the energy of the donor is transferred to the acceptor, so the fluorescence lifetime had a significant downward trend. In the IFE process, there is no energy transfer involved, the amount of excitation light is reduced, but the lifetime of the donor remains relatively constant [39]. This shows that apigenin inhibited the intrinsic recombination of r-CD photogenerated electron-hole pairs, proving the existence of the FRET phenomenon. Fig. 8b shows the UV absorption spectrum of r-CD before and after the addition of apigenin. It is observed that the UV absorption peak at 530 nm was significantly enhanced after the addition of apigenin, which indicates that apigenin enhanced the UV absorption of r-CD at 530 nm, which was also a manifestation of r-CD fluorescence quenching. New absorption peaks were not detected in the spectrum, which was consistent with the dynamic quenching mechanism [22]. In summary, the synergistic effect of FRET and dynamic quenching is most likely to lead to the fluorescence quenching of apigenin by r-CD.

Some progress has been made in the adsorption and enrichment of flavonoids by adsorption method. Lou et al. modified magnetic polystyrene-divinylbenzene microspheres by Mannichi reaction and applied them to purify flavonoids from wolfberry leaves. The maximum adsorption capacity of myricetin reached 81.6 mg g−1 [40]. Wang's team enriched flavonoids with macroporous adsorption resin by ultrasound-assisted method. The total flavonoid extraction rate was 29.2 mg g−1 [41]. In contrast, there are few reports on materials that adsorb apigenin via resins.

The adsorption performance of r-CD@MIP and NIP was analyzed by drawing adsorption isotherms, as shown in Fig. 9. With an increase of the concentration of apigenin (−1), the adsorption growth rate of apigenin by r-CD@MIP continued to accelerate. In the adsorption experiment, when the concentration of apigenin molecules was close to 60 mg L−1, the adsorption equilibrium gradually reached. The maximum adsorption amount of CD@MIP was 28.39 mg g−1, which was significantly greater than the maximum adsorption amount of NIP (14.03 mg g−1). The calculated imprinting factor (I = QMIP/QNIP) was 2.09, indicating the successful imprinting of apigenin by r-CD@MIP. The adsorption mode was further analyzed by fitting the equilibrium adsorption isotherm. As shown in Fig. S3, the results indicated that the adsorption isotherm fitting correlation coefficient of the Freundlich model (R = 0.990) was higher than that of the Langmuir model (R = 0.397), indicating that multilayer adsorption mainly occurred on the material surface.

The kinetic adsorption experiment explored the kinetic adsorption characteristics of r-CD@MIP and NIP for apigenin. The results showed that the adsorption of apigenin by r-CD@MIP and NIP increased rapidly within 75 min, following by a gradual decrease in the adsorption rate and reaching adsorption equilibrium at approximate 90 min (Fig. 9 b). Fitting with pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models was carried out, as shown in Fig. S4. The correlation coefficient of the pseudo-second-order model of r-CD@MIP was R2 = 0.989, which was higher than that of the pseudo-first-order model (R1 = 0.971). This indicated that the adsorption on the surface was dominated by chemical adsorption.

Molecular imprinting technique was adopted to enrich apigenin in the real environment, thereby improving the accuracy of fluorescence detection. The adsorption ability of r-CD@MIP for lake water in real environments was assessed. The apigenin concentration before and after adsorption was determined using a fluorescence method established using r-CD. The adsorption amount of apigenin by r-CD@MIP was 19.7 mg g−1 within 3 h, demonstrating the application capabilities of r-CD and r-CD@MIP in actual environments.

Thiol-containing CDs (r-CD, o-CD, g-CD) emitted three fluorescent colors of red, orange, and green were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using l-cysteine and neutral red as precursors. In addition, r-CD was combined with molecular imprinting technology to prepare a new material r-CD@MIP, demonstrating the following advantages. First, with the precursor proportions held constant, the fluorescence color of the CDs in pure water can be controlled by slightly varying the reaction conditions. Then, the synthesized r-CD and o-CD exhibited excellent fluorescence stability. Even in strong acid and alkali environments, its fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged. Finally, the apigenin detection method was established using r-CD and r-CD@MIP and applied to complex environments and actual samples. r-CD@MIP can be easily separated from the solution by filtration, avoiding material waste and fluorescence contamination, and the entire process complies with green chemistry principles and provides a promising method for the synthesis and application of multicolor CDs.

Qingyan Bai: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shujuan Ma: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Mingjia Yan: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jinshu Wang: Supervision, Resources, Methodology. Xiaoqiong Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Junjie Ou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Ou, and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U2341209) to J. Wang.
